turns.text: -- of the -- of the substantive cause of action.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: No, sir. The amount of the award is neither pled nor approved. The only thing that's pled or approved is an award was made. The amount of it is not admissible in evidence and if the -- if one party or the other files a pleading, it can be stating that the award was zero or $14,000.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where is -- where is your pleading by which you began the suit in the federal court? What did you say about the addendum of the award?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I said that -- that he was asserting a claim against us that we desired to set aside -- it was a formal as -- its on page 1 of the record. In page 3, in -- at paragraph (4), I say that the amount of -- in controversy as claimed by the defendant in which the defendant, which was the employee, claimed before the Board was $14,000 which is a claimed liability against us.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that on your -- on your complaint, you simply said this is a suit under Texas statute and diversity jurisdiction.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And the Texas statute allows a recovery of $14,000 on concept that the jury would bring in $14,000.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. Because when -- when I appealed, the burden of proof is still on him and he is allowed to -- I mean he has burden of going forward and he is still allowed to recover anything that he is claiming. And the award is not even admissible, it's not -- if it's pled, it can be stricken.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But if the Chief Justice's supposition were then taking up by the motion to dismiss or whatever it is in which they say we disavow an amount beyond $9,000, then on the face of the pleading, and you don't contradict -- you -- whether -- that would be controlling, wouldn't be? That'd be a judicial admission.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. Once he comes in and answers and said, "No, we don't claim $14,000."

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: $14,000. He claimed $9,000, then you say the court is --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- is ousted of jurisdiction.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Would -- would that be -- would not -- would that be the case in a -- in a suit that I'm brining for an excess of $10,000? Or --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Pardon me.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Well, could you not judicially -- a plaintiff judicially admit himself out of federal jurisdiction?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Or -- or -- yes, certainly, he could. Forget -- let's forget about workmen's compensation or any diversity case on the contract.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You say that the contract cost for whatever it is, $25,000 adding up to what we're recovering -- potentially covered $25,000. And the defendant said, "I now solemnly say that the outmost of any jury could be simply be fined as $9000 and that's all I'm going to ask the jury." Of course, he'd be thrown out, wouldn't it?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think it would be judicial admission if --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- the person claiming -- claiming the amount of $14,000 later says "No, I'm --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And you say there's no such things are placed here?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: No, sir. He -- he cross it for $14,035 just to see a claime before the Industrial Accident Board. And six days later, they filed a suit in Orange County, Texas for $14,000 for the same amount.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you mean --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: So --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- if he sued for $25,000 -- I'm not quite sure about that, sued for $25,000 for personal injury, after he filed the suit, he comes in and says "I am not going to reduce this. I'm -- I only want to get $9,000." Could he have it removed back to the state court then or have it dismiss for want of jurisdiction? I'm not sure about that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I'm -- I'm not sure either, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I have had some cases whether they denied me that right (Inaudible) [Laughs] for that.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I know in --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't see how you can keep them in the federal court. How can you keep them if you say I now bind myself to not want more than $9000? How could you keep them in the federal court?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I -- I don't know. I know this is true in land cases, Your Honor, that if --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How you could keep them there?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I don't how he could.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I remember they did (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I believe (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Many situations try to remove cases from federal court. Plaintiff didn't like it, files a remittitur, attempts to dismiss, you can't do that with files in remittitur claim in less than the -- the jurisdictional amount but the cases say that once the jurisdiction is vested properly, it's there to the end --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Then I --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- is that right?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- then I was wrong when I answered the Chief Justice --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't he would --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- that counterclaim --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: My practise --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- if that is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- my practice was in those and I had to do it a number of times. To dismiss that suit, you pay the cost and then claim -- they all claim $2999.99.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But I -- I've done it many times. Several time not many times because I didn't make that. It takes time. I hope.[Laughter]

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Cobb --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I don't quite understand because you say this is an original action, a new -- a new action. It's not an appeal from the Industrial Accident Board. It's a new -- it's a new action. Now, you file an action to -- to review this order of $1050 and you do allege in your complaint that such an order was made --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- to that extent, do you not?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: It is --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- jurisdictional --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- but then an order has been made. I --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- describe the amount.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. And by describing the amount.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Alright. Now, you file a -- you file a -- a new suit in the -- in the federal court. And -- is the issue set until you know what the counterclaim will be in that court? And if he files $9,999, isn't that the thing that determines it?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Actually, I don't think so, Your Honor, because of -- of the holding in the Supreme Court of Texas that the amount involved -- that really what this is -- what do you call --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- an appeal is a right of the employee to compensation.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: And what sets his right to how much compensation is the claim that he files. And then regardless of which party appeals, I mean, it -- it doesn't matter --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, you're -- you're using --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- upon suit --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- your appeal to help you where --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Alright, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- formally, you were saying this without --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- an independent action to --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Well, actually, sir --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- to help your position.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- we -- we all -- I mean, the lawyers, they call it an appeal. Actually, the statute never talks of an appeal. It calls of -- before a suit. We --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- but you have to give notice not of -- of appeal but of intention to rather a dissatisfaction with the awards. But we --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- call it by shorthand appeals.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: It is a -- it is a suit and when such a suit is brought to set aside, as the statute calls, the award of the -- or the ruling of the Board, the burden of proof is -- I mean, on the claimant, that is the employee, regardless of who gets in as a nominal plaintiff would offend. The burden of proof is on the claimant to assert and prove his claim. And as the Supreme Court of Texas has stated, the amount of the claim go on the compensation that he is entitled to in such a suit regardless of who files the suit is how much he claim before that Board. If he claimed it less, then he would be entitled to less in such a suit.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He will be entitled what?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: He would not be entitled to anymore than he claimed before the Industrial Accident Board. If he claims --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- a loss of -- loss of use of a little finger, he could not then come in and -- and claim total permanent disability --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- on such an appeal. But when he claims total permanent disability or $14,000 before the Board, and some award is entered and a suit to set it aside has made, then the burden is on -- on the plaintiff regardless of who brings that suit in court to prove his right to compensation and the amount that he is entitled to recover, he is set by his -- set and fixed by the filing of his claim before that Board. And the award never thereafter enters to it. That's why I feel that the petitioner's position that -- that we're merely seeking to escape liability of $1050 award is -- is not true because we are seeking to escape responsibility for the claim or so much is he entitled to $14,000 because once this suit is brought, that award has no place in it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Well, I can -- I can see how -- how perhaps the man wouldn't be permitted to reduce his -- his claim in a lawsuit in order to prevent removal where he had once claimed more than $10,000.But here, you say that it -- it's not removal, it's a brand new suit.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And there, shouldn't he be entitled to claim as much less than the original claim as he wants to claim?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: No more than it -- it's still a granting suit when the employee brings it in federal court which he is allowed to do. And if he brings it for $14,000 and we join issue on that.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: And then he later attempts and say, "I made a mistake in -- in claiming $14,000. I now want to claim $9000 or $5000. I do not think that that would oust jurisdiction of the federal court. I think --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Oh, no --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- it's the same situation here, where we are seeking to relieve ourselves of liability not for $1000 but $14,000. Now, he comes in and says, "No, I -- I really only want $9999." I think you would have to dismiss or counterclaim or -- we would have to -- how would that be? He would or he'd just be there. [Laughter] He would just be there.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Be there but --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- he would be there for less than the jurisdiction amount.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Well, I make it a one -- if once he filed his claim, he attaches this to what the issue between theses two parties.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. I -- I think I have your --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- point, Mr. -- I won't take anymore of your time.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Now if he increase the amount of $15,000.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, $14,050 would --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: But --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: How about your relief? You are asking that the award be set aside. Is that your reason before this Board?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Well, not only the award but that he take nothing by his claim, then he take nothing.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Your -- your liability was $1050 and your prayer was that the relief -- the award be set aside.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible) somewhat $1050 or $1435.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. And that he take -- and -- and plaintiff further phrased that defendant take nothing out and from the plaintiff.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, that's -- that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Well, that --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: [Laughs]

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- obviously follows that the award set aside, isn't it?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: The award could be set aside and he could --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I know, but you haven't vacated, set aside, voided and --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, often not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- declared to be at most further force and effect. I can't imagine. If you got that and that certainly takes nothing.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Well, if it -- he is still entitled to recover some amount of money under his claim, more or less than the award, the award is just a jurisdictional step in -- in the filing of the lawsuit just like the occurrence of the accident as a jurisdictional step.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, what happens in your state practice if -- if he is satisfied with the Board's award and the carrier is not, and you take an appeal to carry or place an appeal to the state court, does this reopen the whole amount more than the $1050 (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. Up to the amount that he may claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Even though he offers no -- nothing more than the evidence that was often before the Board, yet if they come along, the jury comes along with $5000 instead of $1050, this is the risk you take, is it not?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: He is bound by the claim.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: He is bound by the claim that he files but not by the evidence the order -- award --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- of the Board but by the claim that he filed. And since he is bound by it, we feel that that's what attaches the amount in -- between the two courts.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: The claim here was $14,035.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. It was total permanent, $14,035. And our alternative position, we think that jurisdiction attached with the filing of the petition but in the event it didn't, he affirmatively cross-action for that amount of money calling it subject to dismiss but he sought relief to the Court not for any lesser amount than what he claimed before the Board and claimed in a subsequent suit in the state court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: May -- may I ask, Mr. Cobb, I promise you I wouldn't ask you anymore questions --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- but I just want to -- I just want to --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I hope --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- get this in my mind. Suppose you file this suit in the -- in the state court and suppose no counterclaim was filed, what would happen?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: When we tried it with, as I understand it, that -- Your Honor as far as I know this never happen but -- because once -- one of these insurers' appeals, a cross-action is then filed. But if that happen, as I understand it, the claim would be litigated and the burden of proof would be on the -- the employee to establish the extent and duration of his interest. That is my understanding of it. But the form of --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, it's conceivable of the man might be satisfied with the award if --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I would --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- that was given to him and might not feel it necessary or to file a counterclaim. He just -- he just say -- not file any. Now, what would that do about it? Would -- would he -- would they still be opened to --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think in trying --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: If they lose it, I think so, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think if they loss it, they would be.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In a lawsuit, they would be?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think so if he claim that.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In the -- if he claimed it originally before the Board.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think so and --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: -- then a suit bring it aside was -- was brought. I think so. As a practical manner, they always file a cross-action.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, well, now, let's take it in your -- in -- in this case, in the -- in the federal court. Then -- if he haven't filed the $14,000 counterclaim, would he still had been entitled to $14,000 in the federal court if the facts had warranted it?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I think so. Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You do. I -- I just don't know. I have no idea that --

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Because -- because he claimed that same total permanent disability before the State Board, and that is exactly what we were seeking to relieve our liability act.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, if I understand it, if it's a trial de novo in either court, then all is open.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And whatever amount, if any, the evidence sustained is the judgment.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Isn't that right?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. And the Board's award has nothing to do with the --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: So it could be all away from nothing up to the amount stated in the claim of $14,000.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Regardless of who appealed.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: In either court?

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir, state or federal. Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And this could be so whether the redress was sought, the suit we brought either by the claimant or by the employer.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: That's my understanding of it because of the -- the whole purpose of the suit, so-called, to set aside the award is to review how much compensation the claimant is entitled to within the scope of the claim that he made.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, if that is not so, it's not a de novo proceeding.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: That is right, sir. And it is completely de novo. For those -- those reasons and further of the alternative reason that he affirmatively sought $14,000 in his counterclaim, we feel that the decision below should be affirmed.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. VanDercreek.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: We have just one comment to make and reply, Your Honor. You ask where there are any cases where the employee did not file a counterclaim. There's one cited in our brief, in our reply brief on page 2. It is Morris versus Maryland Casualty Company 130 S.W.2d 1080. In that case, the employee did not file a counterclaim, he claimed that the court didn't have -- didn't have -- state court did not have jurisdiction. The court held that jurisdiction was present and he loss out entirely because it's necessary for the employee to come in and counterclaim. The mere filing of the suit by insurance company to set aside the award has the effect of terminating that award. He must appeal from the final award. Now, all these rules concerning the Texas (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So under that case -- under that case, he must file a counterclaim for anything he expects to get, is that right?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: If he wants to get any compensation, he is going to have to file some sort of counterclaim, that is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Do you mean get any compensation or anything in excess of the award?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Under Texas' practices theory will be to actually file a -- a counterclaim. In federal practice, he would probably -- he never says --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Oh, what happened in this case --

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: -- entitled to the Board's award.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- what happened in this case that you cited this truly well (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: In the Morris case --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: -- the Board awarded, as I recall, around $300. The suit was brought in the county court by insurance company to set aside the Board's award. The employee moved to dismiss on grounds that his claim involved more than $300 and that should have brought in the State District Court. The case held and since he had not claimed the specified amount before the Industrial Accident Board, just file the mere claim that the Texas rule in suits -- in that instance, of suits to -- by insurers to set aside the Board's award that the amount in controversy was the amount of the award. They held that the county court had jurisdiction and they also held that the employee because he failed to file a counterclaim would take nothing. The -- all the rules applicable to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I don't follow this -- take nothing, why would take nothing? Not even the $300?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: He ain't going to think, Your Honor, because when the insurance company came in to set aside the Board's award unless he comes in and stands on his two feet and presents his claim, he's all through.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And -- and he wasn't allowed to present his -- his claim (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Well, he just moved to dismiss for want of jurisdiction. And they said -- held that since he failed to file a counterclaim, it was just flock as far as the employee was concern. He was not giving the option of going back in and filing a counterclaim as he attempted to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You mean he was not allowed to offer his evidence in the county court?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And this because he had not filed a counterclaim, is that it?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Yes. He had merely moved to dismiss for want of jurisdiction.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So won by default so to speak, is that it?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Won by default because his motion to dismiss for want of jurisdiction is bad court rule in motion to dismiss want of jurisdiction and holding there was jurisdiction. He chose to rely on his motion of no jurisdiction. He lost and he said in the appellate court, "We're not going to send it back for retrial or permitting to amend."

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Because he has not filed the counterclaim.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: That is precisely correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: It's Morris versus Maryland Casualty Company, 130 S.W.2d 1080. It cites in the reliance upon the Booth case. The Booth case is the Texas case which sets out the various rules and steps and appeals from the Board in amount in controversy.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: May I ask you, Mr. VanDercreek, before you sit down? I understood Mr. Cobb to say that the number of -- of petitions, if that's the right style for compensation in which something in excess of $10,000 is sought is a small portion of the total number compensation claims, this is -- excluding death actions.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: No, I was -- as a matter of fact, I would say that most of the petitions filed with the Board would probably be for -- well, let me say I don't know first of all. But by and large, the attorneys who practice in the workmen's comp -- in Texas usually are trying to file an action for total and permanent disability. Now, even though the person just lost his little finger, he can say besides claiming for loss of the finger, which is specified amount, he can also say that the loss of his finger affected his entire body and now he's totally and permanently disabled. To protect themselves, they were quite often automatically file a maximum, they don't -- total and permanent. They don't have to claim permanent and partial.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, is there also a practice in that stating any amount in the petition?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Yes, there is. Sometimes the workman will just write in a letter requesting a claim where he does not specify any amount before the Board. The Texas rule for the term jurisdiction and a suit by insurance company is the amount of the Board's award. This is all -- I mean, all the Texas rules are summarized in the Booth case. The Fifth Circuit made, in this representation saying regardless who brings a suit, it's the amount in controversy, it's the amount employee claim before the Board. That is not what the Booth case held. It held in a suit by employee to set aside the Board's award. It was not his claim to avoid a privilege in his court petition.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, then, in the Texas -- in the Texas courts, could a man say, "I -- I lost my finger, and I'm therefore permanently and entirely incapacitated," and -- without -- without specifying any amount of -- of damages can then get an award?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Yes, he could. He could file --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And as I understood Mr. Cobb that in those circumstances, if the award was less than the $10,000, the federal court would not have jurisdiction. Did -- did -- I misunderstood you, did I? So don't -- don't bother. If I did misunderstand you, that's alright.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. You could go. You may ask.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: I -- I meant the claim amount there if, for specific injury, let anything less than total and permanent disability or death. In other words, any claims may, or any specific injury, such as the loss of a leg or --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Anything? And -- and there is no permanent and -- and total disability claimed.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And no total and permanent disability is claimed.

turns.name: HOWELL COBB
turns.text: Yes, sir. He's claiming the loss of a leg which is $7000 that is the maximum recovery.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did he --

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: But he claims the loss of a leg, he also claimed total and permanent --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: -- before the Board.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Before you sit down, Mr. VenDercreek. I'd like to ask this question. In -- what is on the plaintiff's original complaint, the insurance case, it says the result -- the -- the claim the sum of $14,000. When your motion to dismiss, you say that the award of $1050, what I want to know is would that statement of the award find any place if the case went to trial either in the state or the federal court?

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: The award which -- which, itself, is -- supersedes the administrative claim is, in itself, set aside by the filing of the suit. When you file a suit to set aside the Board's award --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Would the fact -- I didn't make my question clear. Would the fact that there was an award in amount of $1050, he allowed to appear in the litigation even in the state or federal court.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Mr. Cobb gave absolutely --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: -- the correct answer --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: They don't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: -- neither with the claim filed before the Board. But you have to put --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- pleadings to the jury, I gather in your --

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Not and then ask could you -- it's jurisdictional of the award and the claim, a matter which is explained in the Booth, and you do have to put in your pleadings and as he did, I believe, it's on page -- top of page 3 of the record, asked for it to be set aside.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But the fact of the amount would not appear -- would have to be kept out of the litigation in the court.

turns.name: WILLIAM VANDERCREEK
turns.text: Yes, he cannot tell the jury, "You got an award for such and such." It goes to the court and not to the jury.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well.
